---
title: "Generative AI for Public Health Statistics & Data Science"
subtitle: "Using AI tools safely and effectively in health research"
author: "Erik Westlund"
date: "04/10/2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    transition: slide
    background-transition: fade
    self-contained: true
    css: ../../slides/slides.css
---

```{r}
#| include: false
framework::scaffold()
```

## In The News

- This week Anthropic announced a new model called "Mythos"
- They claim it's not being released to the public because of safety concerns: it has found security vulnerabilities in many major pieces of software
- Questions:

  - Does this raise the stakes for privacy and security in health research?
  - How do we separate out marketing hype from real risk from these companies?

## Learning Goals

- Explain key data-safety risks in AI-assisted workflows
- Use Git and GitHub to move code across environments
- Distinguish chat workflows from agent workflows
- Understand simulation as a safe development strategy

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Generative AI in Health Research: Introduction
:::

## Why We Are Here

- Generative AI tools are increasingly useful for coding and data work
- Their strengths in software tasks do not automatically transfer to statistics
- In health research, **privacy** and **reproducibility** change how we can use these tools
- Today's focus: how to use these tools safely and effectively in health research

## AI History in Two Minutes

- 1950s-1980s: repeated cycles of optimism, brittle systems, and disappointment
- 1990s-2010s: statistical learning becomes more useful than symbolic AI
- 2010s-now: deep learning and large language models produce fluent, general-purpose systems

. . .

The current moment is new in capability, but not new in hype.

## Performance on Tasks

- Frontier models have improved quickly on coding, math, and many benchmark tasks
- Tool use has improved performance further: code execution, retrieval, and agent loops
- In many cases, output quality is noticeably better than even 1 to 2 years ago

## Benchmark Improvement Over Time {.smaller}

::: {.smaller}

| Benchmark | Earlier Strong Result | Recent Frontier Result | Change |
|:----------|:----------------------|:-----------------------|:-------|
| MATH (competition) | 43% | ~96% | +53 pts |
| HumanEval (code generation) | 80% | ~95% | +15 pts |
| MMLU (academic knowledge) | 86% | ~93% | +7 pts |
| SWE-bench Verified | -- | 81% | -- |

:::

## Data Science Benchmarks Matter More Here

- The relevant question for us today is not only whether models can code, but how they can help us in health research and how to use them safely
- Useful tasks include data cleaning, plotting, table generation, debugging, documentation, and method selection

## Generic Coding vs Data Science Tasks {.smaller}

::: {.smaller}

| Task | Type | Performance |
|:-----|:-----|:------------|
| HumanEval code generation | General | 80% |
| MBPP Python problems | General | 74% |
| DS-1000 data science code | Data science | 43% |

:::

. . .

Generic coding improvements do not yet mean that AI models are adept at data science tasks; they are heavily engineered toward general-purpose software engineering work.

## Benchmark Caution: Goodhart's Law

When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.

. . .

- High benchmark scores can hide narrow optimization
- Leaderboard gains may not reflect real world scientific workflows
- Contamination (training on the benchmarks) and benchmark gaming can distort the picture

## AI Weaknesses for Data Science

- Statistical reasoning can still be shallow or inconsistent
- Models can recommend the wrong method confidently
- Generated code can run while implementing the wrong logic; requires auditing
- Produces polished results which can "wow" but be wrong

## Why Software Engineering Adopts AI Faster

- Software tasks often have a built-in feedback loop
- Write code, run tests, and see whether it passes
- This creates a strong surface-level sense of "it works"
- Even there, security, maintainability, and correctness can still be hidden problems

## Why Science Is Often Different

- Scientific work is theory-driven, not only output-driven
- In engineering, it is often plausible to say: when the code can do X it works
- In data science, this is sometimes plausible, such as with data cleaning/prep ("ensure when we do X, the data look like Y")
- However, in analysis, there is rarely any "test" that can prove correctness

## Why Privacy Changes the Workflow

- Health research often involves restricted or identifiable data
- The main risk is not just bad output
- There is a large risk of unauthorized exposure of sensitive information
- This makes workflow design part of the ethics of using AI

## Two Major Risk Surfaces

1. Sending data to third parties in an unauthorized way
2. Leaking data into code repositories

## Chat Interfaces and Agents

- Chat tools usually see what you paste or upload; you control this
- Agents can be given access to data files; this is a powerful but risky capability
- File access changes the privacy risk.

## Privacy: Subscription vs API Access

- AI subscription products and web interfaces may have different data policies than APIs
- API use often has stronger provider commitments around training use (namely, not training on user data)
- Neither subscription nor API access makes it automatically acceptable to send sensitive data
- Policy, contract, institution, and data type still control what is allowed

## Practical Privacy Rule

- It is typically fine to allow AI tools to see code (so long as collaborators have no issue; think "trade secrets")
- You may sometimes allow AI tools to see public or synthetic data
- If sensitive data are present in the project, assume an agent may inspect them unless blocked, and don't trust the AI to avoid seeing them

## Reproducibility and AI Use

- Use AI to help write and revise code that implements the analysis, but not to conduct the analysis itself
- The analysis should be captured in scripts and scientific notebooks
- You should always be able to re-run the analysis from start to finish

## Questions

- Before we proceed, are there any questions about the capabilities, risks, or ethical considerations of using AI tools in health research?

## Common Setup in Health Research: Secure Environment

- It is common to have a secure environment for working with sensitive data (e.g., SAFE Desktop)
- This environment may have restrictions on internet access, software installation, and data movement
- This creates a need for a workflow that allows you to use AI tools outside the secure environment

## Workflow Challenge

- You want to use AI tools to help write code for your analysis
- You have sensitive data that cannot be exposed to AI tools
- You need a way to move code between the AI environment and the secure environment without risking data leakage

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Git and GitHub
:::

## Workflow: Why Git Matters

If code needs to move across environments, you need infrastructure for moving code safely and efficiently. Git provides a way to do this while also creating an auditable record of changes.

## What Git Is

- A version control system
- A local record of changes over time
- A way to inspect what changed, when, and why

## Why Git Matters Here

1. It creates an auditable record of work.
2. It provides affordances like `.gitignore` to reduce accidental data leakage.
3. It uses remotes to make code movement across environments straightforward.

## `.gitignore` as a Safety Tool

- Prevents selected files from being tracked
- Helps keep data, secrets, and temporary files out of repositories
- Does not fix mistakes retroactively

. . .

This is a guardrail, not a substitute for judgment.

## Remotes as Workflow Infrastructure

- Your local repository lives on your machine
- A remote repository can live on GitHub or another server
- This allows you to move code between environments without copying folders around manually

## Secure Environment Pattern

1. Develop code outside the secure environment when appropriate
2. Keep restricted data out of the AI-visible workspace
3. Commit the code
4. Push to a remote
5. Pull the code into the secure environment
6. Run it there on the real data

## Core Principle

Code can move across computing environments; sensitive data cannot.

## Git Demo

- I will first demo this myself
- I am going to use the terminal. Software like RStudio and VS Code have built-in Git interfaces that work similarly.
- We will then do a hands-on activity where you practice the Git workflow and see how it supports safe code movement across environments

## Git Demo: Create a Project Folder

Create a new folder on your machine for this project, and navigate into it in the terminal.

## Git Demo: Create a File

In your editor, create a new file. For this, I'll use "README.md".

Type something and save the file.

## Git Demo: Initialize the Repository

In the terminal, run:

```bash
git init
```

Make sure you are in the project folder when you run this.

## Git Demo: Check Status

```bash
git status
```

Git status is your friend. It tells you what Git sees and what it thinks is going on.

## Git Demo: Create a Repository on GitHub

In your browser:

1. Log in to GitHub.
1. Click `New` next to repositories.
1. Name the repository `[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]`.
1. For practice, make it public.
1. Do not initialize it with a README, `.gitignore`, or license.
1. Click `Create repository`.

## Git Demo: Add a Remote

Replace both bracketed values before you run this.

Here, we use a public repository. In real sensitive work, you will often use a private one.

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME].git
```

## Git Demo: Add the File to Git

```bash
git add {filename}
```

## Git Demo: Commit the File

```bash
git commit -m "Initial commit"
```

`-m` means "message". The message should describe the change you made.

## Git Demo: Push to GitHub

```bash
git push -u origin main
```

- `-u` means `--set-upstream`
- It tells Git to remember that your local `main` branch is connected to `origin/main`

After that, you can usually just run `git push` and `git pull`.

## AI Tip: Git Is a Good AI Task

- AI tools are good at Git commands
- They can help explain what a command does before you run it
- They are useful when you hit routine Git friction

## Workflow Tip: Aliases

- I use a lot of Git aliases to make common commands faster
- I type `s` for `git status`, `ac` for `git add . && git commit -m`, `pullmain` for `git pull origin main`, and so on

## Git Demo: Create `.gitignore`

In your editor, create a new file named `.gitignore`.

## Git Demo: Add a Rule to `.gitignore`

```text
data/
```

## Git Demo: Create an Ignored File

In your editor or the terminal, create a new folder named `data`.

```bash
mkdir data
```

Then create a file like `data/private-data.csv`.

## Git Demo: Confirm It Is Ignored

```bash
git status
```

You shouldn't see a mention of the file. To git, it should not exist.

## Git Demo: Clone Into a Different Directory

We can simulate a secure machine by changing directories and cloning the repository there.

```bash
cd ..
git clone https://github.com/[YOUR-GITHUB-USERNAME]/[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME].git [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
```

## Git Demo: Make a Change in the Copy

```bash
cd [YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]-copy
```

In your editor, create a new file like `notes.md`.

Type something and save the file.

```bash
git add {filename}
git commit -m "Add notes file"
git push
```

## Git Demo: Return to the Original Directory

```bash
cd ../[YOUR-PROJECT-NAME]
```

## Git Demo: Pull the New Change

```bash
git pull
```

## Git Demo: What Just Happened?

- You created a local repository
- You connected it to a remote
- You used `.gitignore` to keep a file out of version control
- You cloned the project elsewhere
- You pushed a change from one copy and pulled it into another

## Private Repo Authentication

- Private repositories require authentication to clone, push, and pull
- Common options are browser sign-in, personal access tokens, and SSH keys
- GitHub Desktop, VS Code, and the GitHub CLI can often simplify this

## Exercise

- Practice the Git workflow with your groups. You can follow the provided PDF handout.

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Harnesses: Chatbots and Agents
:::

## Harnesses

- A harness is the interface around the model
- The model is different from the "harness"
- The model takes in context and returns outputs; the harness interfaces with the model and your files

## Chat

- A standard chat window where you ask questions and get responses back
- You usually have to paste in the context yourself
- Best for brainstorming, explanations, and small pieces of code
- Usually the least connected to your actual project files

## Agent CLI

- An agent that runs in the terminal, such as Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, or Gemini CLI
- Can read files, edit files, search the repository, and run commands

## Agent GUI

- An agent built into an editor or web interface
- Best for people who want agent features in a more visual environment
- In my experience, it can be easier to see what files are actually edited

## Model/Harness Experiment

- I ran an experiment where I gave the same task to multiple model and harness combinations
- The variation in results gives insight into how much the harness and model each contribute to the final output

## Agent Demo

- I will demo an agent CLI and an agent GUI
- We will see how they can read and edit files, and how this creates different privacy risks

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Preserving Privacy with Simulated Data
:::

## Simulated Data as a Privacy Strategy

- One practical strategy is to move structure, not records
- Extract useful information from the private dataset
- Use that information to create a similar practice dataset elsewhere
- Use AI tools on that practice dataset to develop code and workflow

## Step 1: Extract Structure From Private Data

- Variable names
- Data types
- Response categories
- Plausible ranges
- Missingness patterns
- Basic frequencies or counts

## Step 2: Generate Simulated Data

- Create data files with similar variable names and response values
- Preserve the structure needed for cleaning, plotting, and table-making workflows
- Use AI tools on those simulated files outside the secure environment when appropriate

## Simulated vs. Synthetic Data

- Simulated data here means data created to mimic useful structure for workflow development
- Synthetic data often suggests a more formal attempt to preserve the statistical structure of the original data
- For this session, the practical goal is safe code development, not statistical replacement of the original dataset
- R libraries like `synthpop` can be used for more formal synthetic data generation, but they are not necessary for the kind of simulated data we are discussing here

## Limitation

- This is useful for data cleaning, plots, tables, and workflow setup
- It often does not preserve the joint distribution across variables unless you design that explicitly, so it can be less useful for modeling work
- It should not be treated as a substitute for final modeling in the secure environment

## 🚨 Privacy Warning 🚨

- Data can still be identifiable even if you do not move the full dataset
- Free-text fields are especially risky
- Rare values and unusual combinations can still reveal people
- Be careful not to extract PHI or other identifying information

## Demo: Simulated Data Workflow

- I have put several datasets into the `data` folder
- I have also added a Stata `.do` file and an R script that extract crucial information from a dataset that can be useful for simulation, such as variable names, types, and value frequencies
- We will use these materials to practice creating simulated datasets

## Demo

- I will demo this for you, and then we will practice on one of the datasets in the `data` folder
- If you finish early, use the simulated dataset to generate summary statistics, visualizations, or additional exploratory work

## Steps

1. Run the metadata extraction code on a dataset, typically on a secure machine
2. Use the extracted metadata to create a simulated dataset with an LLM

In this case, I will give fewer explicit instructions. In my experience the LLMs are rather good at this task, so I want to give you more freedom to experiment with prompts and see what they can do.

## {background-color="#2c3e50"}

::: {style="color: white; font-size: 1.5em; text-align: center;"}
Workflow Tips for Using LLMs in Health Research
:::

## LLMs Help Automate Things That Used To Be Too "Expensive" to Automate

- LLMs can help you write scripts, aliases, and templates for common tasks
- When you have the thought "Wouldn't it be nice if..." ask yourself if an LLM can help you build that thing
- I do this all the time for workflow, down to having scripts that automate project setup.

## Keep A Paper Trail

- Keep a `docs/` or `notes/` directory for plans, prompts, and decision logs
- Save intermediate findings instead of trusting memory
- Let tools write summaries you can review later

Text files are cheap, searchable, and easy to revisit.

## Write Comments/Documentation You Otherwise Wouldn't

- We are all on tight deadlines
- Documentation is often overlooked or deferred
- Costs you later when you need to revisit
- Use tools to help generate documentation
- Make sure you give yourself time to review these for accuracy and bloat

## Checkpoints and Logs

- Commit after each meaningful step
- Use commits as rollback points when something goes sideways
- Keep short logs or notes so you can resume without rethinking everything

Good workflow reduces the cost of being interrupted.

## Sometimes You Should Just Do It Yourself

- I sometimes find myself working on a data visualization or similar task
- I know how to do it myself
- I spend 3x longer getting mad at an LLM for failing instead of just doing it myself

## Use LLMs to Organize Files

- I have moved away from going first to Zotero to organize literature reviews, notes, etc.
- Everything is a project with a directory
- LLMs can help organize things and summarize
- Not an excuse for reading, but having text of everything can really help you do things faster that would otherwise take forever.

## Use Alternative / Multiple Models To Look For Mistakes

- This is one of the lowest risk, high reward uses for LLMs
- Use a second model to review the work you produced
- Even use a third pass
- Don't trust these are always real mistakes; but even small catches can save you time and embarrassment later

## Use Models to Rubber Duck

- Rubber duck debugging is a classic technique where you explain your code to an inanimate object to find mistakes
- Explain your thinking to a model, even if you don't need it to solve a problem
- One of the biggest benefits of incorporating LLMs is I now spend a lot of time explaining my thoughts and goals, which brings clarity

## Models Aren't Therapists, But They Are Non-judgmental

- We all have gaps in our understanding, and we all make dumb mistakes
- It can be embarrassing to ask for help or admit confusion to colleagues
- LLMs can be a safe way to ask "stupid" questions or get feedback
- All caveats apply: they can be confidently wrong, and they won't know when they are wrong.

## Ask for Explanations

- Ask tools to explain what they are doing
- Catch misunderstandings before they compound
- Use one tool to review another when stakes are higher

Understanding the process is part of the workflow.

## Closing Thoughts

- AI tools can accelerate work and learning, but they are not magic
- Good workflow practices can help you get the most out of these tools while avoiding common pitfalls
- Likewise, LLMs can help improve your workflow

. . .

- *But*: You are responsible for your work, and you should understand it

## Final Questions?

- Are there any final questions about the tools, workflows, or ethical considerations we discussed today?
